section.title: Crossing the Jordan
section.sectionRationale: The promise moves from word to wonder. Israel breaks camp and comes to the flooded Jordan, and God tells them exactly how they will cross: the priests carrying the ark of the covenant will step into the water, and the river will pile up so the whole nation can pass on dry ground. It is one movement, the miraculous crossing that mirrors the Red Sea, exalts Joshua before the people, and proves that the living God is among them.
section.thesis: As at the Red Sea, God parts the waters so His people may pass, but this time the ark of the covenant leads the way into the flood, teaching Israel that they enter the land only behind the presence of the living God, who goes ahead of them to drive out their enemies.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the command to follow the ark and consecrate themselves, verses 1 to 6; God's word exalting Joshua and promising the miracle, verses 7 to 13; and the priests stepping into the flood as the waters heap up and Israel crosses on dry ground, verses 14 to 17.

units.label: Follow the ark and consecrate yourselves
units.note: tomorrow the LORD will do wonders among you

verses.text: Early the next morning Joshua got up and left Shittim with all the Israelites. They went as far as the Jordan, where they camped before crossing over.
After three days the officers went through the camp
and commanded the people: “When you see the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God being carried by the Levitical priests, you are to set out from your positions and follow it.
But keep a distance of about two thousand cubits between yourselves and the ark. Do not go near it, so that you can see the way to go, since you have never traveled this way before.”
Then Joshua told the people, “{{qadash|Consecrate}} yourselves, for tomorrow the LORD will do wonders among you.”
And he said to the priests, “Take the {{aron|ark}} of the covenant and go on ahead of the people.” So they carried the ark of the covenant and went ahead of them.

units.label: God's word exalts Joshua and promises the miracle
units.note: this is how you will know that the living God is among you

verses.text: Now the LORD said to Joshua, “Today I will begin to exalt you in the sight of all Israel, so they may know that I am with you just as I was with Moses.
Command the priests carrying the ark of the covenant: ‘When you reach the edge of the waters, stand in the Jordan.’”
So Joshua told the Israelites, “Come here and listen to the words of the LORD your God.”

He continued, “This is how you will know that the {{chay|living}} God is among you and that He will surely drive out before you the Canaanites, Hittites, Hivites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Amorites, and Jebusites.
Behold, the ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth will go ahead of you into the Jordan.
Now choose twelve men from the tribes of Israel, one from each tribe.
When the feet of the priests who carry the ark of the LORD, the Lord of all the earth, touch down in the waters of the Jordan, its flowing waters will be cut off and will stand up in a heap.”

units.label: The priests step in and Israel crosses on dry ground
units.note: the priests stood firm on dry ground in the middle of the Jordan

verses.text: So when the people broke camp to cross the Jordan, the priests carried the ark of the covenant ahead of them.
Now the Jordan overflows its banks throughout the harvest season. But as soon as the priests carrying the ark reached the Jordan and their feet touched the water’s edge,

the flowing water stood still. It backed up as far upstream as Adam, a city in the area of Zarethan, while the water flowing toward the Sea of the Arabah (the Salt Sea) was completely cut off. So the people crossed over opposite Jericho.

The priests carrying the ark of the covenant of the LORD stood firm on dry ground in the middle of the Jordan, while all Israel crossed over the dry ground, until the entire nation had crossed the Jordan.

qadash.gloss: To consecrate, set apart, make holy. Before God acts, Joshua calls the people to consecrate themselves, to prepare to meet the holy God who is about to do wonders among them. The same call was given at Sinai, Exodus 19:10-15; encountering God's mighty presence always requires that His people be set apart and ready.

aron.gloss: The ark, the sacred chest overlaid with gold that held the tablets of the covenant and was crowned by the mercy seat. As the throne and sign of God's presence among Israel, the aron goes first into the Jordan; the people follow the presence of God into the land. It is the visible pledge that the LORD Himself leads the way.

chay.gloss: Living, alive. Israel will know that the living God is among them, in pointed contrast to the lifeless idols of Canaan. The God who cuts off a flooding river is not a carved image but the living Lord of all the earth, active and present, who will drive out the nations before His people.

basic.context: Israel leaves camp and reaches the Jordan, which is at flood stage during harvest season, a real barrier between them and the land. God gives clear instructions: the priests will carry the ark of the covenant, the sign of God's presence, and step into the river ahead of everyone. When their feet touch the water, the river will stop flowing and pile up, so the whole nation can walk across on dry ground. This is a deliberate echo of the Red Sea crossing under Moses, and God says it will prove two things: that He is with Joshua as He was with Moses, and that the living God is truly among His people. The question the passage answers is: how do God's people enter into what He has promised?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording a real miracle at a real river. It is not a template for expecting God to part rivers for us, but it teaches enduring truths: God leads His people by His presence, He requires them to be consecrated, and He confirms His chosen leaders. The crossing also functions symbolically in Scripture as a passage from the wilderness into the inheritance, a picture later writers draw on.
basic.originalLanguages: Joshua calls the people to qadash, to consecrate themselves, before God works. The aron, the ark, the throne of God's presence, goes first into the flood. And the whole event proves that the chay, the living God, is among them, unlike the dead idols of Canaan.
basic.typology: The ark leading Israel through the waters into the land pictures Christ, God's presence in the flesh, going before His people through death into life. Crossing the Jordan on dry ground, like the Red Sea, is taken up in the New Testament as a picture of baptism and of passing from death to the promised inheritance.
basic.christ: The ark carried the presence of God into the deadly flood so that the people could pass safely; Jesus, God present with us, went down into death itself so that all who follow Him pass safely through. As Joshua was exalted in the sight of Israel, so the Father exalts the greater Joshua, raising Him and setting Him over His people forever.
basic.god: God is the living God, Lord of all the earth, sovereign over the flooding river and over the nations of Canaan. He goes before His people; He requires holiness; and He acts so that all the peoples of the earth may know His mighty hand. The whole crossing is His doing, not Israel's.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 14 tells of the Red Sea crossing this miracle deliberately echoes. Psalm 114 sings of the sea fleeing and the Jordan turning back before the LORD. 1 Corinthians 10 reads Israel's crossings as pictures for the church. And Hebrews 4 shows the land as a shadow of the rest in Christ.
basic.oneStory: God brings His people across the barrier of the Jordan by going before them in the ark, just as He brought them through the Red Sea. It is another decisive step into the promised inheritance, and it points to Christ, God with us, who leads His people through death into the rest that remains.

context.literary: Chapter 3 is built around the ark, mentioned repeatedly, which leads the action from start to finish. The narrative deliberately parallels the Red Sea crossing of Exodus 14, casting Joshua as a new Moses and framing the entry into the land as a second exodus. It uses anticipation skillfully: the instructions are given in full before the miracle, so the reader waits with Israel for the priests' feet to touch the water. The account flows straight into chapter 4, where the memorial stones interpret what happened.
context.historical: The Jordan in the spring, around the barley harvest and the season of Passover, swells and overflows its banks, fed by melting snow from Mount Hermon, so the crossing was humanly impossible at that time. The detail that the water backed up as far as Adam, near Zarethan, some distance upstream, locates the miracle geographically. The ark of the covenant, carried by the Levitical priests, was Israel's most sacred object, the sign of God's throne and presence, so leading with the ark declared that God Himself led the invasion. Gilgal, where they would camp, lay just inside the land opposite Jericho.
context.canonical: The Jordan crossing stands with the Red Sea as one of the two great water-partings that bracket Israel's wilderness journey, the exodus out and the entrance in. Psalm 114 celebrates both together. The New Testament draws on this imagery: passing through the water, following God's appointed leader, entering the inheritance. Joshua's exaltation before Israel, confirming that God was with him as with Moses, establishes his authority for the conquest and typologically anticipates the exaltation of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record God's miraculous parting of the flooded Jordan so Israel could enter the land, to confirm Joshua as God's appointed leader in the line of Moses, and to teach that Israel enters the promise only behind the presence of the living God, who leads and who must be met in holiness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a unique, unrepeatable miracle at a specific moment in redemptive history; it does not promise that God will part waters for believers or prescribe a ritual to copy. What endures is the pattern it reveals: God goes before His people by His presence, calls them to consecration, and acts to make His power known so that all the earth may know Him and His people may fear Him.

debates.question: Why did God have the ark go first into the river rather than simply parting the water as at the Red Sea?

views.label: To center the whole event on God's presence
views.case: By sending the ark, the throne of God's presence, ahead into the flood, God makes unmistakably clear that it is His presence, not Joshua's staff or Israel's strength, that opens the way; the people follow God into the land, and the priests stand in the riverbed holding the ark until all have passed, a vivid picture of the LORD Himself bearing His people across.

views.label: To require a step of faith before the miracle
views.case: Unlike the Red Sea, where the water parted before Israel moved, here the priests must step into the flooding river first, and only then does the water heap up; the sequence teaches that God's people advance by faith, acting on His word before they see the deliverance.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge: the crossing magnifies God's leading presence and calls His people to follow that presence in faith, trusting the promise before the waters part.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key words all point to the presence and holiness of God. Before anything happens, Joshua commands the people to qadash themselves, to consecrate, to be set apart, for one does not meet the holy God casually; the same summons was given at Sinai before God descended, Exodus 19. Then the aron, the ark, moves to the front. The ark was the golden chest that held the covenant tablets and was crowned by the mercy seat, the throne and visible sign of God's presence among His people; sending it first declares that the LORD Himself is leading the invasion, and the people simply follow His presence into the land. And the sign of the whole miracle is that the chay, the living God, is among them, a deliberate jab at the lifeless idols of Canaan: the God who stops a river in flood is no carved block of wood but the living Lord of all the earth, who will surely drive out the seven nations before His people. Consecrate, ark, living God: the vocabulary makes the crossing a lesson about the God who is present, holy, and alive.

typology.type: The ark, God's presence, going first into the deadly flood
typology.fulfillment: Christ, God with us, going before His people into death itself so they pass through safely, Hebrews 2:14-15 and John 11:25.

typology.type: Israel crossing the waters into the inheritance
typology.fulfillment: The people of God passing through the waters, a pattern the New Testament connects to baptism and to entering the promised rest, 1 Corinthians 10:1-2.

typology.type: God exalting Joshua before all Israel
typology.fulfillment: The Father exalting the greater Joshua, raising and enthroning Christ over His people, Philippians 2:9-11.

study.christ: The heart of this miracle is that God's presence went first into the deadly waters so that His people could cross in safety. The priests bore the ark, the throne of the living God, down into the flooded Jordan and stood there until every last Israelite had passed. It is a picture of the gospel: Jesus, Immanuel, God with us, went down into the deep waters of death and judgment, and He remained there, bearing it, so that all who follow Him pass through from death into life, John 11:25-26. As the crossing echoed the exodus, so Christ accomplishes a greater exodus, leading His people out of bondage and into the inheritance. And just as the LORD exalted Joshua in the sight of all Israel to confirm His chosen leader, so the Father has highly exalted Jesus, raising Him and giving Him the name above every name, that we might know God is with Him and follow Him into the land of rest.
study.god: The God of this chapter is the living God, the Lord of all the earth, and everything bows before Him. He commands a river in full flood and it stands up in a heap; He promises to drive out seven nations stronger than Israel; He confirms His chosen leader with a public sign. He goes before His people by His own presence in the ark, so that the entry into the land is His work from first to last, not Israel's achievement. He is holy, requiring His people to consecrate themselves before they follow Him. And He acts for a purpose that reaches beyond Israel: so that all the peoples of the earth may know that His hand is mighty, and that His own people may fear Him always. This is a God who is present, powerful, holy, and self-revealing.

crossReferences.note: The Red Sea crossing under Moses, deliberately echoed here as Israel again passes through the waters on dry ground.
The sea fled and the Jordan turned back before the presence of the LORD, celebrating both crossings together.
God fulfills His promise to be with Joshua as He was with Moses, exalting him before the people.
Israel's passage through the water is read as a pattern with meaning for the New Testament people of God.
The land Israel enters is a shadow of the greater rest that remains in Christ.

study.oneStory: God brings His people across the impassable Jordan by going before them in the ark, mirroring the Red Sea and marking a second exodus into the promised land. The living God, Lord of all the earth, opens the way by His own presence and confirms His chosen leader. It is a decisive step into the inheritance sworn to Abraham, and it points beyond the land to Christ, God with us, who goes before His people through death and leads them into the rest that lasts forever.

quiz.q: What went ahead of the people into the Jordan?

options.text: The ark of the covenant, carried by the priests, the sign of God's presence.
options.note: Verses 6 and 11 to 17 make the ark leading the way the center of the crossing.

options.text: Joshua alone, holding up his staff over the water.
options.note: Moses raised a staff at the Red Sea; here it is the ark of God's presence that leads, not Joshua's staff.

options.text: The armed men of the eastern tribes as a vanguard.
options.note: The eastern tribes did cross armed, but what led the way into the river was the ark.

options.text: A pillar of fire moving over the river.
options.note: The pillar guided in the wilderness; here God's presence leads through the ark.

quiz.q: When did the waters of the Jordan pile up?

options.text: As soon as the priests carrying the ark stepped into the water's edge.
options.note: Verses 15 to 16 show the water stopped only after the priests stepped in, a step of faith.

options.text: The night before, so the riverbed was dry by morning.
options.note: The crossing happened as they advanced, not the night before; the water parted when the priests stepped in.

options.text: After the whole nation had already begun wading across.
options.note: The water heaped up first, when the priests' feet touched it, so the people could cross on dry ground.

options.text: Only after Joshua struck the river with a rod.
options.note: No rod is struck; the water stood still when the priests bearing the ark stepped in.

quiz.q: What did the miracle prove, according to Joshua?

options.text: That the living God was among them and would drive out the nations before them.
options.note: Verse 10 states the crossing would show the living God was present and powerful to give the land.

options.text: That Joshua was a greater leader than Moses had been.
options.note: It showed God was with Joshua as with Moses, confirming him, not that he surpassed Moses.

options.text: That Israel's engineers had mastered the river.
options.note: It was a divine miracle, not human skill; the point was the living God among them.

options.text: That the Canaanite gods were also powerful.
options.note: The opposite: the living God is contrasted with the lifeless idols of the nations.

quiz.q: Why did God call the people to consecrate themselves first?

options.text: Because they were about to meet the holy God who would do wonders among them.
options.note: Verse 5 ties consecration to encountering God's mighty, holy presence, as at Sinai.

options.text: Because they needed to rest before the hard crossing.
options.note: Consecration is about holiness before God, not physical rest before exertion.

options.text: Because they had to purify the river before entering it.
options.note: They consecrated themselves, not the river; the point was readiness to meet the holy God.

options.text: Because consecration had nothing to do with the crossing.
options.note: It was directly tied to it: consecrate yourselves, for tomorrow the LORD will do wonders.

quiz.q: How does the crossing point to Christ?

options.text: God's presence went first into the deadly flood so His people could pass, as Christ went into death so His people pass to life.
options.note: The ark bearing God's presence into the waters foreshadows Christ, God with us, John 11:25.

options.text: It shows that God only helps those strong enough to cross on their own.
options.note: The crossing was wholly God's doing; the point is God leading, not human strength.

options.text: It teaches that the land itself is the final goal of salvation.
options.note: The land is a shadow of the greater rest in Christ, not the final goal.

options.text: It has no bearing on Christ or the gospel.
options.note: The ark leading through the waters clearly foreshadows Christ leading His people through death to life.

questions: The priests had to step into the flooding river before the waters parted. Where is God calling you to act on His promise before you can see how it will work out?
Israel followed the ark, God's presence, into a way they had never traveled before. How does it steady you to know that God goes ahead of you into the unknown?
Joshua called the people to consecrate themselves before God acted. What might it look like to prepare your heart to meet God rather than approaching Him casually?
God worked the miracle so that all the peoples of the earth would know His mighty hand. How does remembering that God's purposes are for His glory reshape the way you pray about your own needs?

The ark carried God's presence into the deadly waters ahead of the people, foreshadowing Christ going into death for us. How does Jesus going before you into the hardest places give you courage this week?

translationNotes.note: The BSB sets off the Lord of all the earth with em dashes; these have been rendered as commas here, with no change to the wording.
Two thousand cubits is about 3,000 feet or 914 meters, as the BSB footnote notes; the gap kept the holy ark visible yet set apart from the people.
The Sea of the Arabah, the Salt Sea, is the Dead Sea, as the BSB footnote notes; the water backed up far upstream near the city of Adam.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 3:1-17. Prose narrative. The em dashes in verse 13 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
